intro_text:
Praia do Cassino runs along the southernmost stretch of the Brazilian coast, in Rio Grande do Sul, and it is most often introduced with a superlative: the longest beach in the world. The sand is unbroken from the Molhes, the great stone jetties at the entrance to the Rio Grande seaport in the north, all the way south to the mouth of the Chuí Stream on the border with Uruguay, a distance sources put at anywhere from 212 to 254 kilometers. Guinness World Records recognized it as the world's longest beach in 1994.

The claim deserves an asterisk, and the honest version is more interesting than the slogan. Beach length runs straight into the coastline paradox: the more finely you measure a shoreline's every curve, the longer it gets, so 'longest beach' is partly an artifact of how you measure. By some readings the 80-Mile Beach in Western Australia is longer. What is not in dispute is that Cassino is one of the longest uninterrupted sandy beaches on Earth, long enough that the usual idea of a beach, a place you walk from end to end, simply does not apply.

It is also not a tropical postcard, and never pretended to be. This is cold water: the Falklands (Malvinas) Current runs north up this coast, and the South Atlantic here is grey and bracing, not Caribbean turquoise. The wind is relentless. And the beach is a working surface, not just a leisure one. The firm sand doubles as a road; people drive cars and motorbikes straight onto it and along it, with the standing advice to use four-wheel drive and check the tide before setting out, because the same flat hardness that makes it drivable will strand and then drown a vehicle caught by the rising sea.

At the northern end, the Molhes da Barra define the place. These breakwaters of piled stone reach far out to protect the entrance of the Rio Grande port, and a wind-powered sail-cart runs about 4.3 kilometers out along the jetty to the lighthouse in roughly 20 minutes, a genuinely strange and lovely piece of local engineering. Sixteen kilometers south of town, the rusting hull of the Altair has sat stranded on the sand since a storm drove it ashore in June 1976. Sea lions haul out along the coast and are visited by boat. And every so often the whole impossible length of the beach becomes a racecourse: the Cassino Ultra Race runs 230 kilometers down it.

What Cassino offers is not prettiness but scale and honesty. It is a vast, cold, windswept, working immensity of sand, one of Brazil's oldest beach resorts at its developed northern end and effectively empty wilderness for most of its length, where the headline is a contested record and the reality is something better: a beach so long it stops being a beach and becomes a coastline you could lose a day driving down.
honest_reckoning_note:
The honest thing to say about Cassino's headline is that 'the longest beach in the world' is a claim, not a measurement. Beach length is governed by the coastline paradox: shorelines have no single true length, because the more closely you trace their curves the longer they become, so any 'longest' title depends on the resolution you measure at and on where you decide one beach ends and the next begins. Cassino's figures range from 212 to 254 kilometers across sources, and rivals like Western Australia's 80-Mile Beach have their own claims. Guinness gave Cassino the title in 1994; that is a real recognition, but it is not a settled physical fact, and the fairer statement is that Cassino is among the longest uninterrupted sandy beaches on Earth.

The second honesty is about what the beach actually is, against the romance of 'world's longest.' Most of those hundreds of kilometers are not idyll but empty, wind-scoured, cold-water wilderness backed by a working seaport, and the beach's role as a road is a real hazard, not a quirk: vehicles get stuck in soft patches and caught by the incoming tide. This is a place that rewards respect over enthusiasm. Its appeal is precisely that it is not a manicured resort strand but a raw, elemental coast where the South Atlantic, the Falklands Current, and the wind set the terms, and where the rusting Altair has been allowed to simply sit and corrode for nearly fifty years as part of the scenery.
reckoning_pullquote: 'The longest beach in the world' is a claim, not a measurement; the honest version is a coastline so long it stops behaving like a beach.

day_in_time.dawn: At dawn the beach is immense and nearly empty, the sand hard and grey-gold, the wind already up off a cold sea. Surf-fishers work the shorebreak and the first vehicles appear as specks far down the strand. The Molhes stand black against the early light at the northern end.
day_in_time.midday: By midday, in the southern-hemisphere summer, the developed northern stretch near Cassino town fills with bathers braving the cold water, while cars and motorbikes run along the firm sand in both directions. A few kilometers south the crowds thin to nothing and the beach becomes a road through emptiness.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon stretches shadows down a beach with no visible end. The low sun catches the rusting hull of the Altair and turns the wet sand to pewter. This is when the sheer length of the place registers, a shoreline running to the horizon and past it toward Uruguay.
day_in_time.night: After dark the beach is vast and black, the lighthouse on the Molhes turning at the north end, the wind and cold surf the only constant. The town's lights stop quickly; beyond them is one of the longest stretches of unlit, empty sand anywhere on the planet.

food_drink.name: Cassino town and Rio Grande
food_drink.description: The developed northern end at Cassino town and the nearby city of Rio Grande hold the restaurants and bars, serving southern-Brazilian fare and Atlantic seafood. Most eating happens here; the rest of the beach is unserviced wilderness.
food_drink.where: Cassino town and Rio Grande (~18 km)

food_drink.name: Gaúcho food
food_drink.description: This is gaúcho country, the cattle south of Brazil, so churrasco (barbecue) and the strong-brewed chimarrão (yerba mate) are the regional staples you will find in Rio Grande, distinct from the food of tropical Brazil to the north.
food_drink.where: Rio Grande and Rio Grande do Sul region

food_drink.name: Carry your own beyond the town
food_drink.description: Once you drive south of the developed stretch there is nothing, no kiosks, no services, for tens of kilometers. Anyone heading down the beach should carry water, food, fuel, and recovery gear and not count on finding anything.
food_drink.where: The undeveloped length of Praia do Cassino

timeline.title: Rio Grande is founded
timeline.description: The city and port of Rio Grande, the oldest city in Rio Grande do Sul, is founded in 1737. The beach is the seafront of this historic southern port, and the port's entrance defines the beach's northern end.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Rio Grande, Rio Grande do Sul

timeline.title: Cassino opens as a beach resort
timeline.description: On 26 January 1890 Praia do Cassino debuts as a tourist complex, developed by the Suburban Mangueira Company under Antônio Cândido Sequeira, making it one of the oldest beach resorts in Brazil.

timeline.title: The Molhes da Barra
timeline.description: In the early twentieth century the great stone breakwaters, the Molhes da Barra, are built out into the sea to protect the entrance of the Rio Grande port. They fix the beach's northern end and become its signature structure.
timeline.source: Port of Rio Grande history (early 20th c.)

timeline.title: The Altair runs aground
timeline.description: In June 1976 the ship Altair is driven ashore by a strong storm and strands on the beach about 16 kilometers south of the town center, where its rusting hull has remained ever since as a landmark.

timeline.title: Recognized as the world's longest beach
timeline.description: Guinness World Records recognizes Praia do Cassino as the world's longest beach in 1994, the title that has defined its fame, even as the coastline paradox keeps the claim contested.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Praia do Cassino; Guinness World Records

zones.name: The Molhes da Barra
zones.character: The northern end, where the stone breakwaters reach far out to protect the Rio Grande port entrance. A wind-powered sail-cart runs about 4.3 km out along the jetty to the lighthouse in roughly 20 minutes, one of the beach's defining sights.
zones.best_for: The jetty, the sail-cart ride, the lighthouse, watching ships enter the port
zones.notes: The Molhes fix the beach's northern boundary and the harbor mouth.

zones.name: Cassino town beach
zones.character: The developed northern stretch fronting Cassino town, the only part with hotels, restaurants, and summer crowds. One of Brazil's oldest beach resorts, busy in the southern-hemisphere summer and quiet otherwise.
zones.best_for: Services, summer bathing (cold water), the practical base
zones.notes: Effectively all of the beach's infrastructure is concentrated here.

zones.name: The Altair and the long south
zones.character: South of town the beach becomes near-empty wilderness running for hundreds of kilometers toward Uruguay. About 16 km down sits the rusting hull of the Altair, stranded since 1976; beyond it the sand is a drivable road through emptiness.
zones.best_for: The shipwreck; the experience of the beach's sheer scale; driving (with care)
zones.notes: No services beyond town; 4WD and tide-awareness essential for driving south.

landmarks.name: Molhes da Barra
landmarks.description: The great stone jetties built out into the sea to protect the entrance of the Rio Grande port, with a wind-powered sail-cart running ~4.3 km along the western breakwater to the lighthouse. The defining structure at the beach's northern end.

landmarks.name: Wreck of the Altair
landmarks.description: The hull of the ship Altair, driven ashore in a June 1976 storm and stranded about 16 km south of the town center, left to rust in place ever since as the beach's best-known landmark south of the resort.

landmarks.name: Port of Rio Grande
landmarks.description: Brazil's southernmost major seaport, founded with the city of Rio Grande in 1737. Its entrance, guarded by the Molhes, marks the northern terminus of the beach.

cultural_refs.title: The 'world's longest beach' title
cultural_refs.description: Cassino's defining claim to fame, recognized by Guinness World Records in 1994 and repeated worldwide, even though the coastline paradox keeps the 'longest' status genuinely contested.

cultural_refs.title: The wreck of the Altair
cultural_refs.description: The stranded ship that has become an accidental monument, sitting and rusting on the sand since 1976, a fixed point on an otherwise featureless immensity of beach.

cultural_refs.title: The Cassino Ultra Race
cultural_refs.description: A 230-kilometer ultramarathon run down the beach itself, an event that exists only because the beach is long enough to hold it, with shorter 73 km and 135 km options.

recurring_events.name: Cassino Ultra Race
recurring_events.description: A 230 km ultramarathon (with 73 km and 135 km distances) run along the length of the beach, using the very feature, extreme length, that makes Cassino famous.

recurring_events.name: The southern-hemisphere summer season
recurring_events.description: The Brazilian summer is when the developed northern stretch at Cassino town fills with vacationers; the rest of the year the long beach is largely empty and wind-scoured.

things_to_know.label: Cold water, not tropical
things_to_know.note: The Falklands (Malvinas) Current makes the South Atlantic here cold and grey, not Caribbean. Expect bracing water and persistent wind; this is the cool, wild south of Brazil, not the tropical northeast.

things_to_know.label: Driving the beach is real, and risky
things_to_know.note: The firm sand serves as a road and vehicles drive on it legally, but use four-wheel drive, check the tide before setting out, and do not go far without recovery gear: cars get bogged in soft patches and caught by the incoming tide.

things_to_know.label: Beyond the town there is nothing
things_to_know.note: All services are at the developed northern end. South of Cassino town the beach is empty wilderness for tens of kilometers toward Uruguay; carry water, food, and fuel and tell someone your plan.

things_to_know.label: Getting there
things_to_know.note: The beach fronts Rio Grande, in the far south of Brazil. The nearest airport is in Pelotas (PET), about 55 km away; Rio Grande itself is about 18 km from the beach. It is a long way from Brazil's main tourist centers.

access_note: Praia do Cassino is the seafront of Rio Grande, in the far south of Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil. The nearest airport is João Simões Lopes Neto International (PET) in Pelotas, about 55 km away; the city of Rio Grande is about 18 km from the developed beach. The Molhes and Cassino town anchor the northern end; the beach then runs south, largely empty, toward the Uruguay border.
spike_explainer:
Praia do Cassino is sold on a single line, the longest beach in the world, and the honest unpacking of that line is the best way to understand the place. The sand runs unbroken from the Molhes, the stone jetties at the mouth of the Rio Grande seaport, south to the Chuí Stream on the Uruguay border, a distance variously given as 212 to 254 kilometers. Guinness World Records recognized it as the world's longest beach in 1994. It is, by any measure, one of the longest uninterrupted sandy beaches on Earth.

**The asterisk is the interesting part.** 'Longest beach' collides with the coastline paradox: a shoreline has no single true length, because the more finely you trace its curves the longer it measures, so any record depends on the resolution you use and on where you rule that one beach ends and another begins. That is why Cassino's own figures swing by 40 kilometers between sources, and why rivals such as Western Australia's 80-Mile Beach have credible competing claims. The defensible statement is not 'the longest' but 'among the longest, long enough that the ordinary concept of a beach breaks down.' You do not walk Cassino end to end. You drive it, or you run an ultramarathon down it.

**It is cold, and it is a working coast.** This is the cool, wind-scoured south of Brazil, not the tropical northeast. The Falklands Current carries cold water north along this shore, the South Atlantic is grey and bracing, and the wind rarely stops. The beach is also a working surface as much as a leisure one: its firm sand functions as a road, and people drive cars and motorbikes directly onto and along it. That is not a novelty so much as a genuine hazard, with standing advice to use four-wheel drive and check the tide, because vehicles get bogged in soft patches and caught by the rising sea on a beach with no high ground to retreat to.

**The Molhes.** The northern end belongs to the Molhes da Barra, breakwaters of piled stone built out into the sea in the early twentieth century to protect the entrance of the Rio Grande port. They are the beach's signature structure, and they come with one of its strangest pleasures: a wind-powered sail-cart that runs about 4.3 kilometers out along the western jetty to the lighthouse in roughly 20 minutes. It is a piece of local engineering with no real equivalent elsewhere, a sailing vehicle on rails over the sea.

**The wreck.** Sixteen kilometers south of town, the hull of the Altair has sat on the sand since a storm drove it ashore in June 1976. Nearly half a century later it is still there, rusting in place, an accidental monument and a fixed landmark on a beach that otherwise offers the eye almost nothing to hold onto. Sea lions haul out along this coast and are visited by boat, and once a year the Cassino Ultra Race sends runners 230 kilometers down the strand, an event that can exist only because the beach is absurdly long.

**The history.** None of this is new. Rio Grande, the oldest city in the state, was founded in 1737, and Cassino opened as a tourist complex on 26 January 1890, under the Suburban Mangueira Company, making it one of the oldest beach resorts in Brazil. The developed northern end at Cassino town, busy in the southern-hemisphere summer with around 150,000 visitors a year, is a real and old seaside resort; it just happens to be the doorway to a few hundred kilometers of near-empty wilderness.

**Why it matters.** Cassino is worth a page not because it is the prettiest beach, it is decidedly not, but because it is the most honest kind of superlative: a place whose fame rests on a contested record and whose reality is better than the record. Strip away the Guinness line and you still have a vast, cold, windswept, working coastline backed by a centuries-old port, drivable for hundreds of kilometers, marked by a rusting ship and a sailing cart on the sea, running south until it quietly becomes Uruguay. It is less a beach than a coastline you could lose a day to, and that, not the title, is the reason to go.

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: Rio Grande, Rio Grande do Sul — far south of Brazil

key_facts.label: Length
key_facts.value: 212–254 km (sources vary), port to Uruguay border

key_facts.label: Claim to fame
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Praia do Cassino; Guinness World Records

key_facts.label: Founded
key_facts.value: 26 January 1890 — one of Brazil's oldest beach resorts

key_facts.label: The Molhes
key_facts.value: Stone jetties + 4.3 km wind-powered sail-cart to the lighthouse

key_facts.value: The Altair, stranded on the sand since June 1976

key_facts.value: Firm sand serves as a road; 4WD + tide-check to drive it

margin_notes.text: The 'longest beach' title runs into the coastline paradox: a shoreline's measured length grows the more finely you trace it, so the record depends on resolution and on where one beach is judged to end. Cassino's own figures vary by ~40 km.

The Falklands (Malvinas) Current carries cold water north along this coast, part of the energetic Brazil–Falkland Confluence. The water is grey and bracing, not tropical, and the wind is near-constant.

The firm sand is a legal road, but driving it is a real hazard: vehicles bog in soft patches and get caught by the incoming tide. Four-wheel drive, a tide check, and recovery gear are the standing advice.

The Molhes da Barra, built in the early 20th century to protect the Rio Grande port entrance, anchor the beach's north end. A wind-powered sail-cart runs ~4.3 km out along the western jetty to the lighthouse.
Rio Grande is the oldest city in the state (founded 1737); Cassino opened as a resort on 26 January 1890. The developed north end draws ~150,000 visitors a year, mostly in the December–March summer.
